items.title: Build sales and purchase registers from submitted documents only
items.description: The sales register and purchase register each project submitted invoices for one company into lightweight register rows carrying document number, party, net total, tax total, grand total, status and posting date. Restricting to submitted status means the register reconciles to what has actually been posted to the general ledger.
items.applicability: The base listing reports from which most subledger and tax figures are derived.
items.example: SalesRegister(company) returns one row per submitted sales invoice; a draft invoice never appears.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: filter on company and submitted status in the query and project to a record rather than loading full invoice entities. Test implication: assert a register lists exactly the submitted invoices and excludes drafts. Honest boundary: registers are flat document-level listings — no line-level drill-down or running balance is produced.

items.title: Summarise by party with count and total that reconcile to the register
items.description: Sales-summary-by-customer and purchase-summary-by-supplier group submitted invoices by party and emit a row per party with the invoice count and the summed grand total. The sum of all party totals must equal the sum of the corresponding register's grand totals, because both read the same submitted set.
items.applicability: Party-level analytics layered directly on the register set.
items.example: Summing every customer row's total from SalesSummaryByCustomer equals summing GrandTotal across SalesRegister for the same company.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: group by the party key in EF and select count and sum; do not group by a constant. Test implication: assert the party-summary total reconciles to the register total. Honest boundary: the summary aggregates grand totals only — it does not break figures into net versus tax per party.

items.title: List outstanding invoices by remaining amount
items.description: Outstanding-sales-invoices and outstanding-purchase-invoices list submitted documents whose outstanding amount is greater than zero, projecting the outstanding amount as the grand-total figure on each row. This isolates the unpaid portion of the ledger for collections and payables follow-up.
items.applicability: Receivables and payables follow-up reporting, and the input set for aging.
items.example: Before any payment, a freshly submitted sales invoice appears in OutstandingSalesInvoices with its full amount outstanding.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: filter on outstanding amount greater than zero and carry the outstanding amount, not the original grand total, into the row. Test implication: assert an unpaid submitted invoice appears and is excluded once fully settled. Honest boundary: outstanding is the stored running figure on the invoice — partial-payment allocation detail is not reproduced in this report.

items.title: Bucket receivables aging into 0-30 / 31-60 / 61-90 / 90+ by invoice age
items.description: Receivables aging takes outstanding submitted sales invoices, computes each invoice's age in days from posting date to an as-of date (defaulting to now), and accumulates the outstanding amount into one of four buckets: 0-30, 31-60, 61-90, and over 90 days. Each party row reports the four buckets plus a total that equals their sum.
items.example: A set of freshly posted invoices lands entirely in the 0-30 bucket, so each party's 0-30 figure equals their total.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: materialise the small outstanding set first, then bucket in memory so the day-difference logic is exact and testable. Test implication: assert recent invoices place their full total in the 0-30 bucket and that buckets sum to the total. Honest boundary: aging is by invoice posting date — there is no separate due-date or payment-terms-driven aging yet.

items.title: Reconcile tax as output minus input to a net figure
items.description: The tax summary sums the tax total of submitted sales invoices as output tax, sums the tax total of submitted purchase invoices as input tax, and reports the net as output minus input. Output tax must equal the sum of the sales register's tax totals, so the report reconciles to the registers.
items.applicability: Periodic tax position reporting and reconciliation against the sales and purchase registers.
items.example: Output tax equals the summed TaxTotal of the sales register; net tax equals output tax minus input tax.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: sum tax totals over submitted documents with a null-safe aggregate and compute net as a single subtraction. Test implication: assert output tax reconciles to the sales register and net equals output minus input. Honest boundary: this is a single combined tax position — there is no per-rate, per-jurisdiction, or filing-period breakdown.

items.title: Value stock from the latest ledger balance per item and warehouse
items.description: Stock valuation finds each distinct item-and-warehouse combination that has stock-ledger activity for the company, asks the stock service for that combination's current balance (quantity and value), and emits a valuation row, dropping combinations that net to zero quantity and zero value. The figures therefore come from the authoritative moving-average ledger, not a separate cache.
items.applicability: Inventory valuation reporting and the basis for the reorder report.
items.example: After receiving 100 and selling 20 of an item, stock valuation shows quantity 80 with the corresponding moving-average value.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive distinct item/warehouse pairs from the ledger and read each balance from the stock service so valuation always matches the ledger. Test implication: assert valuation reflects received-minus-issued quantity per item. Honest boundary: valuation is point-in-time from the latest balance — no FIFO layers, batch, or serial valuation is exposed.

items.title: Flag items at or below a reorder threshold
items.description: The reorder report reuses stock valuation and returns the rows whose on-hand quantity is at or below a supplied threshold. This gives a simple, parameterised low-stock alert built on the same authoritative balances as valuation, with no separate counting logic to drift out of sync.
items.example: With 80 on hand, the item appears in a reorder report run at threshold 90 but not at threshold 10.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: layer the reorder filter on top of the valuation result rather than re-querying, so the two reports cannot disagree. Test implication: assert an item shows at a threshold above its quantity and is absent at a threshold below it. Honest boundary: the threshold is a caller-supplied number — there is no per-item reorder level, safety stock, or lead-time calculation.

items.title: Summarise work orders grouped by production status
items.description: The work-order summary groups a company's production orders by status and emits a row per status with the order count and the total quantity in that status. Because it reads the live order status, it tracks the manufacturing pipeline as orders advance from Draft through to Completed.
items.applicability: Manufacturing pipeline reporting and WIP visibility at the status level.
items.example: One Draft order for 5 units shows a Draft row with count 1 and total quantity 5; after completion the same order appears under Completed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: group by the status enum in EF and select count and quantity sum; do not group by a constant. Test implication: assert a draft order appears under Draft and moves to Completed after the lifecycle runs. Honest boundary: the summary counts orders by status only — it does not value WIP or report material consumption.

items.title: Scope every report to one company
items.description: Every report query filters on company identifier, so a report run for one company never leaks another company's documents or stock. Requesting an unknown company returns an empty result rather than an error, making company isolation a structural property of the reporting layer.
items.applicability: Multi-company data isolation across all reports.
items.example: SalesRegister and StockValuation for a non-existent company id both return empty lists.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: include the company filter in the base query of every report method, never as an afterthought applied in memory. Test implication: assert reports for an unknown company id are empty. Honest boundary: scoping is by company only — there is no branch, cost-centre, or row-level user filter on the reports themselves.

items.title: Aggregate with grouping keys, never group-by-constant
items.description: All aggregating reports group by a meaningful key (party, status) or sum with a null-safe aggregate, and never use a group-by-constant projection. This both produces correct multi-row results and avoids the SQL Server aggregation pathology that has historically caused page hangs in the platform.
items.applicability: Any report that counts or sums across rows.
items.example: Sales-summary groups by customer id; tax summary uses a null-safe Sum; neither collapses all rows under a constant group key.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: prefer separate scalar aggregates or explicit grouping keys over GroupBy on a constant. Test implication: assert aggregating reports return one row per real key and reconcile to their source register. Honest boundary: this is a query-shape discipline, not a guarantee of index coverage or performance at very large volumes.

items.title: Deliver depth as query reports, not a BI surface
items.description: The reporting layer provides a broad set of service methods returning typed rows for registers, summaries, aging, tax, valuation, reorder and work orders. These are consumable by UI or API but are deliberately query-and-record reports rather than an interactive analytics product.
items.applicability: Setting expectations for what the generated reporting layer covers versus a dedicated reporting tool.
items.example: A controller can call ReceivablesAging and render the buckets in a table; there is no pivot, drill-through, or scheduled export built in.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: expose each report as a focused method returning a typed record list so callers compose presentation themselves. Test implication: assert each report returns the expected typed rows for seeded activity. Honest boundary: there is no BI dashboard, chart engine, print/PDF designer, or scheduled-distribution capability — these are query reports only.
